attributes.stem: A 58-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with acute abdominal pain and undergoes a laparotomy, washout and oversewing of a perforated peptic ulcer. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. His weight is 80 kg. There were no complications during surgery and minimal blood loss. A urinary catheter drains 40 mL/hr over the first three hours after surgery. His serum biochemistry was normal immediately before the operation.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following fluid regimens for maintenance fluid would be most appropriate over the next 24 hours?
attributes.explanation:
When considering intravenous fluid therapy, the purpose of the therapy needs to be considered. Typically, IV fluids are commenced for:

  1. Fluid resuscitation,

  2. Routine maintenance, or

  3. Replacement and redistribution.

Resuscitation may be indicated in a haemodynamically unstable patient (eg. hypotensive, tachycardic, delayed CRT, cool peripheries, tachypnoeic). Routine maintenance involves ensuring the patient's standard daily fluid and electrolyte requirements are met. Replacement and redistribution is fluid therapy for complex patients where there may be significant ongoing losses (eg. vomiting, diarrhoea, NG tube, drains, stomas), electrolyte abnormalities, or known cardiac, hepatic or renal disease.

In the immediate post-operative period, fluid requirements and decision making will be made on an individual basis, taking in to account the patient's overall health and the nature of the surgical procedure. The fluid therapy for this patient would include both routine maintenance fluid AND replacement and redistribution - fluid status would be monitored and assessed frequently, with particular attention paid to fluid losses (urine, drains, NG tube, and intra-abdominally) and serum biochemistry to inform ongoing appropriate management.

In respect of ONLY the routine maintenance for this patient, the most appropriate fluid regimen needs to consider both the volume requirements and the electrolyte needs.

  • Water: Normal daily requirements are 25–30 ml/kg/d water. Assuming an average patient weight of 80kg, approximately 2000-2400ml of fluid will be required in a 24-hour period.

  • Electrolytes: Normal daily requirements are 1 mmol/kg/day of sodium, potassium, and chloride. Assuming an average patient weight of 80kg, approximately 80mmol of sodium, potassium and chloride will be required in a 24-hour period.

  • Glucose: Requirements are 50-100g of glucose in a 24-hour period.

Whilst approximately 2000ml of fluid per day may be sufficient, studies have demonstrated that more restrictive post-operative fluid therapy results in increased rates of oliguria and acute kidney injury, when compared to more liberal fluid therapy. Considering this, the surgery that the patient has just undergone, and that our patient already has a 'low' normal urine output (normal 1.5-2.0ml/kg/hr), it would be more appropriate to prescribe a higher volume of fluid as to prevent potential kidney injury. A knowledge of the constituents of the various readily available fluids is also required. See below a list of some common fluids (Image). Referencing this table, we can determine the most appropriate fluid(s) to meet our patient's daily requirements.

As such, the 2L/24hr responses would be appropriate. Of the 3L/24hr regimens, two have too little potassium and one has too much sodium chloride.

2L isotonic saline + 1L dextrose 5% + 30 mmol potassium provides insufficient potassium supplementation for daily requirements.

1L isotonic saline + 1L dextrose 5% provides insufficient total volume and no potassium supplementation.

3L Hartmann's solution provides adequate volume but contains only 5 mmol/L of potassium, which is insufficient for daily requirements. It also lacks glucose supplementation.

1L isotonic saline + 1L dextrose 5% + 60 mmol potassium provides insufficient total volume for maintenance requirements.

1L isotonic saline + 2L dextrose 5% + 60 mmol potassium provides approximately 154 mmol of both sodium and chloride, potassium close to the required 80 mmol and 100g of glucose. This is the most appropriate response. Whilst the sodium and chloride requirements will exceed his daily needs, this patient has normal renal function and should be able to handle this moderate excess amount of electrolyte.

However, remember that this patient will be closely monitored, with serum biochemistry performed shortly after surgery, and fluid and electrolytes will need to be titrated to any fluid losses and biochemical disturbances. Third space losses need to be considered, apart from those from nasogastric and other drains.
learningPoints: Routine maintenance requirements - water is 25–30 ml/kg/day, sodium/chloride/potassium are 1mmol/kg/day each, glucose is 50-100g/day
Regular monitoring of serum electrolytes are vital in the guiding of appropriate fluid therapy for patients
references: Intravenous Fluid Therapy. Fluid therapy in adults in hospital (CG174). National Institute of Clinical Excellence (NICE). 2013;1, updated 2016. URL: https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/cg174/evidence/full-guideline-pdf-191667999 (Accessed March 2022)

Myles PS, Bellomo R, Corcoran T, Forbes A, Peyton P, Story D, Christophi C, Leslie K, McGuinness S, Parke R, Serpell J. Restrictive versus liberal fluid therapy for major abdominal surgery. New England Journal of Medicine. 2018 Jun 14;378(24):2263-74.
